title: Galerkin-finite difference method for fractional parabolic partial differential equations
author: Hossan, Datta, et al.

steps_raw.title: Method details
steps_raw.instruction: We provide the first Bernoulli polynomials and their modified versions to use as basis functions for the weighted residual methods throughout this paper.

steps_raw.title: Galerkin method for space fractional PDE
steps_raw.instruction: We solve the Eq. (3) with the help of the Galerkin Weighted Residual method. In this method, basis functions need to meet the homogeneous boundary requirements. And initial condition is another important step in solving any kind of partial differential equation. In this case, we select initially a trial solution. Let us consider such an approximate solution of the Eq. (3) as(5)uňú(x,ŌĄ)=ő¶0(x,ŌĄ)+‚ąĎj=1naj(ŌĄ)ő¶j(x) where ő¶0(x,ŌĄ) satisfies the non-homogeneous part of the boundary conditions, ő¶j(x) denotes the basis functions which must fulfill the homogeneous boundary requirements, and the unknown parameters, aj(ŌĄ) are function of time [44].

steps_raw.title: Galerkin method for space fractional PDE
steps_raw.instruction: Now the residual function is(6)R(x,ŌĄ)=‚ąāuňú‚ąāŌĄ‚ąíq(x)‚ąāő≤uňú‚ąāxő≤‚ąís(x,ŌĄ)Substituting the Eq. (5) into (6) we get,(7)‚ąāuňú‚ąāŌĄ=‚ąĎj=1ndaj(ŌĄ)dŌĄő¶j(x)+‚ąāő¶0(x,ŌĄ)‚ąāŌĄand(8)‚ąāő≤uňú‚ąāxő≤=‚ąĎj=1naj(ŌĄ)dő≤ő¶j(x)dxő≤+‚ąāő≤ő¶0(x,ŌĄ)‚ąāxő≤Then the weighted residual equation is(9)‚ąę01R(x,ŌĄ)ő¶i(x)dx=0where, ő¶i(x) are the basis functions defined in (2) for all i=1,2,...,n.

steps_raw.title: Galerkin method for space fractional PDE
steps_raw.instruction: Substituting (7) and (8) into Eq. (6), then (9) leads us,(10)‚ąę01(‚ąĎj=1ndaj(ŌĄ)dŌĄő¶j(x)+‚ąāő¶0(x,ŌĄ)‚ąāŌĄ‚ąíq(x)(‚ąĎj=1naj(ŌĄ)dő≤ő¶j(x)dxő≤+‚ąāő≤ő¶0(x,ŌĄ)‚ąāxő≤)‚ąís(x,ŌĄ))ő¶i(x)dx=0

steps_raw.title: Galerkin method for space fractional PDE
steps_raw.instruction: Now(11)‚ąĎj=1ndaj(ŌĄ)dŌĄ‚ąę01ő¶j(x)ő¶i(x)dx+‚ąę01‚ąāő¶0(x,ŌĄ)‚ąāŌĄő¶i(x)dx‚ąí‚ąĎj=1naj(ŌĄ)‚ąę01(dő≤ő¶j(x)dxő≤)ő¶i(x)q(x)dx‚ąí‚ąę01(‚ąāő≤Ōē0(x,ŌĄ)‚ąāxő≤)ő¶i(x)q(x)dx‚ąí‚ąę01s(x,ŌĄ)ő¶i(x)dx=0 which can be rewritten as(12)‚ąĎj=1ndaj(ŌĄ)dŌĄ‚ąę01ő¶j(x)ő¶i(x)dx‚ąí‚ąĎj=1naj(ŌĄ)‚ąę01(dő≤ő¶j(x)dxő≤)ő¶i(x)q(x)dx=‚ąę01(‚ąāő≤ő¶0(x,ŌĄ)‚ąāxő≤)ő¶i(x)q(x)dx‚ąí‚ąę01‚ąāő¶0(x,ŌĄ)‚ąāŌĄő¶i(x)dx+‚ąę01s(x,ŌĄ)ő¶i(x)dxWe can define the integrals of the above equation in terms of matrix. The dimension of the matrix depends on the number of parameters or basis functions. In matrix notation, the Eq. (12) reduces to(13)[C]{da(ŌĄ)dŌĄ}+[K]{a(ŌĄ)}={F}where,Cij=‚ąę01ő¶j(x)ő¶i(x)dxKij=‚ąí‚ąę01(‚ąāő≤‚ąāxő≤ő¶j(x))ő¶i(x)q(x)dxFi=‚ąę01(‚ąāő≤ő¶0(x,ŌĄ)‚ąāxő≤q(x)‚ąí‚ąāő¶0(x,ŌĄ)‚ąāŌĄ+s(x,ŌĄ))ő¶i(x)dx

steps_raw.title: Galerkin-Finite Difference method for time-space fractional PDE
steps_raw.instruction: This method is a combination of discretization of time fractional terms [34], and the modified Galerkin method for space fractional terms. Now we are ready to provide the mathematical formulation for the Eq. (4) explicitly. For this, we introduce the discretized approximation of the time-fractional derivative [34] as:‚ąāőĪu(x,ŌĄn+1)‚ąāŌĄőĪ=(őīŌĄ)‚ąíőĪőď(2‚ąíőĪ){u(x,ŌĄn+1)‚ąíu(x,ŌĄn)+‚ąĎl=1n(u(x,ŌĄn+1‚ąíl)‚ąíu(x,ŌĄn‚ąíl))((l+1)(1‚ąíőĪ)‚ąíl(1‚ąíőĪ))} which is equivalent to,(14)‚ąāőĪu(x,ŌĄn+1)‚ąāŌĄőĪ=V[u(x,ŌĄn+1)‚ąíu(x,ŌĄn)+‚ąĎl=1n(u(x,ŌĄn+1‚ąíl)‚ąíu(x,ŌĄn‚ąíl))Pl]where, Pl=((l+1)(1‚ąíőĪ)‚ąíl(1‚ąíőĪ)), V=(őīŌĄ)‚ąíőĪőď(2‚ąíőĪ) and time step size as, őīŌĄ=ŌĄN

steps_raw.title: Galerkin-Finite Difference method for time-space fractional PDE
steps_raw.instruction: After applying Galerkin method, we calculate u(x,ŌĄn) at different values of x, and at any specified time step, ŌĄn which is defined for n=1,2,...,N.

steps_raw.title: Galerkin-Finite Difference method for time-space fractional PDE
steps_raw.instruction: We choose the approximate solution as,(15)uňú(x,ŌĄn)=ő¶0(x,ŌĄn)+‚ąĎi=1kai(ŌĄn)ő¶i(x)where, j represents the number of parameters. Unlike as Galerkin method, ő¶0(x,ŌĄn) calculates the non-homogeneous part of the boundary conditions at any specified value of time, ŌĄn,ai(ŌĄn) are the unknown parameters whose values are needed to be determined again at the specified time, ŌĄn and ő¶i(x) are basis functions such as we have discussed in (2). Now we define the residual function asR(x,ŌĄn+1)=V[uňú(x,ŌĄn+1)‚ąíuňú(x,ŌĄn)+‚ąĎl=1n(uňú(x,ŌĄn+1‚ąíl)‚ąíuňú(x,ŌĄn‚ąíl))Pl]‚ąíq(x)[‚ąāő≤uňú(x,ŌĄn+1)‚ąāxő≤]‚ąís(x,ŌĄn+1)To find a set of linear equations, we consider:(16)‚ąę01R(x,ŌĄn+1)ő¶j(x)dx=0wherej=1,2,...,kor equivalently,(17)‚ąę01(V[uňú(x,ŌĄn+1)‚ąíuňú(x,ŌĄn)+‚ąĎl=1n(uňú(x,ŌĄn+1‚ąíl)‚ąíuňú(x,ŌĄn‚ąíl))Pl]‚ąíq(x)[‚ąāő≤uňú(x,ŌĄn+1)‚ąāxő≤]‚ąís(x,ŌĄn+1))ő¶j(x)dx=0

steps_raw.title: Galerkin-Finite Difference method for time-space fractional PDE
steps_raw.instruction: After substitution of uňú from Eq. (15) into Eq. (17) we get,‚ąę01(V[(ő¶0(x,ŌĄn+1)+‚ąĎi=1kai(ŌĄn+1)ő¶i(x))‚ąí(ő¶0(x,ŌĄn)+‚ąĎi=1kai(ŌĄn)ő¶i(x))+‚ąĎl=1n((ő¶0(x,ŌĄn+1‚ąíl)+‚ąĎi=1kai(ŌĄn+1‚ąíl)ő¶i(x))‚ąí(ő¶0(x,ŌĄn‚ąíl)+‚ąĎi=1kai(ŌĄn‚ąíl)ő¶i(x)))Pl]‚ąíq(x)[‚ąāő≤(ő¶0(x,ŌĄn+1)+‚ąĎi=1kai(ŌĄn+1)ő¶i(x))‚ąāxő≤]‚ąís(x,ŌĄn+1))ő¶j(x)dx=0or,V[‚ąę01((ő¶0(x,ŌĄn+1)+‚ąĎi=1kai(ŌĄn+1)ő¶i(x))ő¶j(x))dx‚ąí‚ąę01((ő¶0(x,ŌĄn)+‚ąĎi=1kai(ŌĄn)ő¶i(x))ő¶j(x))dx+‚ąĎl=1n(‚ąę01((ő¶0(x,ŌĄn+1‚ąíl)+‚ąĎi=1kai(ŌĄn+1‚ąíl)ő¶i(x))ő¶j(x))dx‚ąí‚ąę01((ő¶0(x,ŌĄn‚ąíl)+‚ąĎi=1kai(ŌĄn‚ąíl)ő¶i(x))ő¶j(x))dx)Pl]‚ąí‚ąę01q(x)[‚ąāő≤(ő¶0(x,ŌĄn+1)‚ąāxő≤+‚ąĎi=1kai(ŌĄn+1)‚ąāő≤(ő¶i(x))‚ąāxő≤]ő¶j(x)dx‚ąí‚ąę01s(x,ŌĄn+1)ő¶j(x)dx=0 or,(18)V[‚ąę01((ő¶0(x,ŌĄn+1)‚ąíő¶0(x,ŌĄn))ő¶j(x)dx+‚ąĎi=1k(ai(ŌĄn+1)‚ąíai(ŌĄn))‚ąę01ő¶i(x)ő¶j(x)dx+‚ąĎl=1n(‚ąę01((ő¶0(x,ŌĄn+1‚ąíl)‚ąíő¶0(x,ŌĄn‚ąíl))ő¶j(x)dx+‚ąĎi=1k(ai(ŌĄn+1‚ąíl)‚ąíai(ŌĄn‚ąíl))√ó‚ąę01ő¶i(x)ő¶j(x)dx)Pl]‚ąí‚ąę01q(x)(‚ąāő≤(ő¶0(x,ŌĄn+1))‚ąāxő≤)ő¶j(x)dx‚ąí‚ąĎi=1kai(ŌĄn+1)√ó‚ąę01q(x)‚ąāő≤(ő¶i(x))‚ąāxő≤ő¶j(x)dx‚ąí‚ąę01s(x,ŌĄn+1)ő¶j(x)dx=0

steps_raw.title: Galerkin-Finite Difference method for time-space fractional PDE
steps_raw.instruction: We evaluate the values of unknown parameters, ai(ŌĄn) after substituting the value of n in Eq. (18). For example, if we substitute n=0, we obtain linear equations at ŌĄ=őīŌĄ. Solving those equations we find out an approximate solution at a specified time value as a function of x such as uňú(x,őīŌĄ). Repeating the steps for n=1,2,...,N, we will be able to calculate the values of u(x,ŌĄ) at different x for any value of time. We express the integrals in terms of matrix and in matrix notation,(19)V[[G]{a(ŌĄn+1)‚ąía(ŌĄn)}+‚ąĎl=1n({H}+[G]{a(ŌĄn+1‚ąíl)‚ąía(ŌĄn‚ąíl)})Pl]=[K]{a(ŌĄn+1)}+{M}whereGij=‚ąę01ő¶i(x)ő¶j(x)dxHj=‚ąę01((ő¶0(x,ŌĄn+1‚ąíl)‚ąíő¶0(x,ŌĄn‚ąíl))ő¶j(x)dxKij=‚ąę01q(x)(‚ąāő≤‚ąāxő≤ő¶i(x))ő¶j(x)dxMj=‚ąę01(q(x)‚ąāő≤(ő¶0(x,ŌĄn+1))‚ąāxő≤‚ąíV(ő¶0(x,ŌĄn+1)‚ąíő¶0(x,ŌĄn))+s(x,ŌĄn+1))ő¶j(x)dx

steps_raw.title: Method validation
steps_raw.instruction: To simplify the convergence analysis of the Galerkin-Finite Difference method, we consider the following fractional diffusion equation in both space and time but without a source term and a constant diffusion coefficient (K).(20)‚ąāőĪu‚ąāŌĄőĪ=K‚ąāő≤u‚ąāxő≤If we write the residual function setting to zero we get,(21)V[uňú(x,ŌĄn+1)‚ąíuňú(x,ŌĄn)+‚ąĎi=1n(uňú(x,ŌĄn+1‚ąíi)‚ąíuňú(x,ŌĄn‚ąíi))pi]=K[‚ąāő≤uňú(x,ŌĄn+1)‚ąāxő≤]Particularly, for n=0, (ŌĄ1=őīt) the Eq. (21) can be written as,(22)uňú(x,ŌĄ1)‚ąíKV[‚ąāő≤uňú(x,ŌĄ1)‚ąāxő≤]=p0uňú(x,ŌĄ0)and, in general, we get the following equation,(23)uňú(x,ŌĄn+1)‚ąíKV[‚ąāő≤uňú(x,ŌĄn+1)‚ąāxő≤]=(1‚ąíp1)uňú(x,ŌĄn)+pnuňú(x,ŌĄ0)+‚ąĎi=1n‚ąí1(pi‚ąípi+1)uňú(x,ŌĄn‚ąíi)

Therefore, the convergence order of the scheme is given by|uňúmn‚ąíu(xm,ŌĄn)|‚Č§C(őīŌĄ)where,n=1,2,...,Nandm=1,2,...,M‚ąí1.

steps_raw.title: Method validation
steps_raw.instruction: Throughout this, we have exploited C as a positive constant with (0.5<C<2) depending on the problem. The following theorem is derived for the case, 0<őĪ<1. Based on the above discussion and literature [[34], [45]], we can state the following similar theorem:

steps_raw.instruction: Theorem: Consider the approximate solution as uňú(xm,ŌĄn) of the exact solution u(xm,ŌĄn), and for simplification, we choose, uňú(xm,ŌĄn)=uňúmn and u(xm,ŌĄn)=umn. Then there is always a positive constant C such that,|uňúmn‚ąíumn|‚Č§C(őīŌĄ)where,n=1,2,...,Nandm=1,2,...,M‚ąí1.

steps_raw.title: Method validation
steps_raw.instruction: For stability analysis, we insert (14) into (20) to get(24)V[u(x,ŌĄn+1)‚ąíu(x,ŌĄn)+‚ąĎi=1n(u(x,ŌĄn+1‚ąíi)‚ąíu(x,ŌĄn‚ąíi))pi]=K[‚ąāő≤u(x,ŌĄn+1)‚ąāxő≤]On using (15), (21) and (24) we can write(25)V[u(x,ŌĄn+1)‚ąíuňú(x,ŌĄn+1)‚ąí(u(x,ŌĄn)‚ąíuňú(x,ŌĄn))+‚ąĎi=1n(u(x,ŌĄn+1‚ąíi)‚ąíuňú(x,ŌĄn+1‚ąíi)‚ąí(u(x,ŌĄn‚ąíi)‚ąíuňú(x,ŌĄn‚ąíi)))pi]=K[‚ąāő≤u(x,ŌĄn+1)‚ąāxő≤‚ąí‚ąāő≤uňú(x,ŌĄn+1)‚ąāxő≤]

(26) becomes(27)V[Em1‚ąíEm0]=K‚ąāő≤‚ąāxő≤(Em1).We consider L‚ąě norm of the error defined as ‚ą•E1‚ą•‚ąě=|Ej1|,wherexj=max(xm), and (27) leads us(28)‚ą•E1‚ą•‚ąě=|Ej1|‚Č§|Ej1|‚ąíKV(‚ąāő≤‚ąāxő≤|Ej1|)=|Ej0|‚Č§‚ą•E0‚ą•‚ąě.For n=1, the Eq. (26) reduces to(29)V[Em2‚ąíEm1+(Em1‚ąíEm0)p1)]=K‚ąāő≤‚ąāxő≤(Em2).Using the notation ‚ą•E2‚ą•‚ąě=|Ej2|, we obtain.(30)‚ą•E2‚ą•‚ąě=|Ej2|‚Č§|Ej2|‚ąíKV(‚ąāő≤‚ąāxő≤|Ej2|)=|(1‚ąíp1)Ej1+p1Ej0|‚Č§(1‚ąíp1)|Ej0|+p1|Ej0|‚Č§‚ą•E0‚ą•‚ąěSimilarly, by using method of induction we can show that for any l-th time step,(31)‚ą•El+1‚ą•‚ąě‚Č§‚ą•E0‚ą•‚ąěThus, we may conclude that the finite difference of the time fractional term is unconditionally stable, and details are available in [34].

steps_raw.title: Method validation
steps_raw.instruction: Now, we consider four problems, including two applications that appear in Financial Mathematics. Here we define an absolute error, L‚ąě-norm and order of convergence, respectively, as follows,AbsoluteError=|uňú(x,t)‚ąíu(x,t)|L‚ąě-norm=max(Ai),whereAiaretheabsoluteerrors.OrderofConvergence,C(őīt)=e1e2where, e1 and e2 represents L‚ąě- norm for different time step size.

(3) and is taken from [32], on a specified domain, 0 0and,u(x,0)=x3.The exact solution is:u(x,t)=x2(1‚ąíx)e‚ąítUsing the mathematical formulation described for space fractional PDE with two parameters let us approximate the solution in the form:(33)uňú(x,t)=‚ąĎi=12ai(t)ő¶i(x).We obtain the following residual function:(34)R(x,t)=‚ąāuňú(x,t)‚ąāt‚ąí(q(x)‚ąā1.8uňú(x,t)‚ąāx1.8+s(x,t))and the residual equation force us(35)‚ąę01R(x,t)ő¶j(x)dx=0where,j=1,2or,‚ąĎi=12dai(t)dt‚ąę01ő¶i(x)ő¶j(x)dx‚ąí‚ąĎi=12ai(t)‚ąę01(d1.8dx1.8ő¶i(x))ő¶j(x)q(x)dx‚ąí‚ąę01s(x,t)ő¶j(x)dx=0In matrix notation, we define,(36)[C]{da(ŌĄ)dŌĄ}+[K]{a(ŌĄ)}={F}where,Cij=‚ąę01ő¶j(x)ő¶i(x)dxKij=‚ąí‚ąę01(‚ąā1.8‚ąāx1.8ő¶j(x))ő¶i(x)q(x)dxFi=‚ąę01s(x,ŌĄ)ő¶i(x)dx

steps_raw.title: Method validation
steps_raw.instruction: Here, ő¶1(x)=x2‚ąíx, ő¶2(x)=x2‚ąí3x22+x3. Hence, we obtain,

steps_raw.title: Method validation
steps_raw.instruction: In [46], shifted Chebyshev polynomials of the second kind were used to solve the problem. Abbas and Mehdi solved the space fractional diffusion equation using a numerical technique based on the shifted Legendre tau method in [30]. In [31], the fractional diffusion equation is reduced to a system of ordinary differential equations by using the properties of Chebyshev polynomials. In [32], the collocation method using the fourth kind of Chebyshev polynomial and compact finite difference method has been used. Now we compute approximate values and absolute errors using two parameters which are displayed in Table 1, Table 2, and Table 3 at different values of time. In these tables, we compare our obtained results with the existing values [30], [31], [32], [46]. These tables show that the Galerkin method provides us with better accuracy for various values of x, and we may observe that the Galerkin approach has outperformed the existing methods described in [30], [31], [32], [46]. steps_raw.title: Method validation
steps_raw.instruction: Problem-2: We are considering the Eq. (4) with time and space fractional diffusion equation as [45]:(37)‚ąāőĪu‚ąātőĪ=‚ąāő≤u‚ąāxő≤+f(x,t) for 0<őĪ<1 and 1<ő≤<2, andf(x,t)=őď(3+őĪ)2x2(x‚ąí1)t2‚ąí2x(2‚ąíő≤)t(2+őĪ)[3xőď(4‚ąíő≤)‚ąí1őď(3‚ąíő≤)],with the boundary and initial conditions: u(0,t)=u(1,t)=0 and u(x,0)=0. The exact solution for this problem is, u(x,t)=x2(x‚ąí1)t(2+őĪ)

steps_raw.title: Method validation
steps_raw.instruction: Since both time and space contain fractional derivatives we have fixed the values of derivative order such as őĪ=0.2andő≤=1.6 for this problem. To start with the solution to this problem, as basis functions, we have chosen only two modified Bernoulli polynomials. Due to the presence of fractional time derivative, we have solved this problem using the Galerkin- Finite Difference method. At first, we choose an approximate solution for this problem in the following form:(38)uňú(x,ŌĄn)=ő¶0(x,ŌĄn)+‚ąĎi=12ai(ŌĄn)ő¶i(x) where, Ōē0(x,ŌĄ) represents non-homogeneous part of our boundary condition and Ōē0(x,ŌĄn) displays it‚Äôs value at any specified time. Since we already have homogeneous boundary conditions so we can have, Ōē0(x,ŌĄn)=0. So, our new approximate solution becomes,(39)uňú(x,ŌĄn)=‚ąĎi=12ai(ŌĄn)ő¶i(x)To approximate the solution of Eq. (37) using the Galerkin method we solve the following integration, and obtain a set of linear equations,(40)‚ąę01R(x,ŌĄn+1)ő¶j(x)dx=0Putting the residual function into Eq. (40) we get‚ąę01(V[uňú(x,ŌĄn+1)‚ąíuňú(x,ŌĄn)+‚ąĎl=1n(uňú(x,ŌĄn+1‚ąíl)‚ąíuňú(x,ŌĄn‚ąíl))Pl]‚ąí[‚ąāő≤uňú(x,ŌĄn+1)‚ąāxő≤]‚ąíf(x,ŌĄn+1))ő¶j(x)dx=0Using Eq. (39) in the above equation we can obtain,V[‚ąĎi=12ai(ŌĄn+1)‚ąę01(ő¶i(x)ő¶j(x))dx‚ąí‚ąĎi=12ai(ŌĄn)‚ąę01(ő¶i(x)ő¶j(x))dx+‚ąĎl=1n(‚ąĎi=12ai(ŌĄn+1‚ąíl)‚ąę01(ő¶i(x)ő¶j(x))dx‚ąí‚ąĎi=12ai(ŌĄn‚ąíl)‚ąę01(ő¶i(x)ő¶j(x))dx)Pl]‚ąí‚ąĎi=12ai(ŌĄn+1)‚ąę01[‚ąāő≤(ő¶i(x))‚ąāxő≤]ő¶j(x)dx‚ąí‚ąę01(f(x,ŌĄn+1)ő¶j(x))dx=0For, n=0 we get two linear equations as following:(41)V[‚ąę01(ő¶12(x))dx(a1(ŌĄ1)‚ąía1(ŌĄ0))+‚ąę01(ő¶1(x)ő¶2(x))dx(a2(ŌĄ1)‚ąía2(ŌĄ0))]=‚ąę01(ő¶12(x))dx(a1(ŌĄ1))+‚ąę01(ő¶1(x)ő¶2(x))dx(a2(ŌĄ1))+‚ąę01(f(x,ŌĄ1)ő¶1(x))dx(42)V[‚ąę01(ő¶1(x)ő¶2(x))dx(a1(ŌĄ1)‚ąía1(ŌĄ0))+‚ąę01(ő¶22(x))dx(a2(ŌĄ1)‚ąía2(ŌĄ0))]=‚ąę01(ő¶1(x)ő¶2(x))dx(a1(ŌĄ1))+‚ąę01(ő¶22(x))dx(a2(ŌĄ1))+‚ąę01(f(x,ŌĄ1)ő¶2(x))dxNow we substitute the values of unknown parameters (a1,a2) at initial time state (ŌĄ0=0) from the given initial condition via following the steps discussed below:(43)‚ąę01(uňú(x,0)‚ąíu(x,0))ő¶j(x)dx=0Applying the approximate value from Eq. (39) into (43) we get,(44)‚ąę01(‚ąĎi=12ai(0)ő¶i(x)‚ąí0)ő¶j(x)dx=0From Eq. (44) we get a set of linear equations which provide us the values of unknowns. Repeating the process for increasing time steps we obtain our desired result. For this problem, we take the basis functions as:

steps_raw.title: Method validation
steps_raw.instruction: The solution of this problem is not similar to Eq. (37) due to the discretization of the time-fractional derivative. So, our approximated solution is represented at t=1 as, u(x,1)=1.01x3‚ąí1.02x2+0.0127x and at t=0.5 as, u(x,0.5)=0.219x3‚ąí0.222x2+0.00323x

steps_raw.title: Method validation
steps_raw.instruction: Now, we compute absolute error and order of convergence to compare the results with the existing literature [47], which are shown in Tables 4 and 5 for different values of őĪ and ő≤.Table 4Error analysis for Problem-2 for őĪ=0.4 and ő≤=1.75.Table 4őītFor őīx=11000 in [45]Present method for őīx=110Absolute ErrorConvergence order C(őīt)Absolute ErrorConvergence order C(őīt)1208.535166√ó10‚ąí05-4.521834√ó10‚ąí03-1402.901776√ó10‚ąí051.55653.495112√ó10‚ąí031.29381809.718379√ó10‚ąí061.57812.553417√ó10‚ąí031.368811603.176376√ó10‚ąí061.61331.804433√ó10‚ąí031.4151Table 5Error analysis for Problem-2 for őĪ=0.5 and ő≤=1.88.Table 5őītFor őīx=11000 in [45]Present method for őīx=110Absolute ErrorConvergence order C(őīt)Absolute ErrorConvergence order C(őīt)1201.326761√ó10‚ąí04-5.600296√ó10‚ąí03-1404.803713√ó10‚ąí051.46574.587591√ó10‚ąí031.22071801.722465√ó10‚ąí051.47973.550801√ó10‚ąí031.291911606.114506√ó10‚ąí061.49422.660682√ó10‚ąí031.3345

steps_raw.title: Method validation
steps_raw.instruction: To analyze the convergence order we have considered maximum absolute error for different time steps and displayed them in Table 6, and we observe that the order of convergence is ensured. The tabular representation of absolute errors for Problem-2 is shown in Table 7 and Table 8. We generate Table 7 and Table 8 by changing one of the parameters (őĪ, ő≤) and keeping fixed the other. According to the tables, for fixed ő≤=1.6 we get better values of u(x,t) at őĪ=0.2. and for fixed őĪ=0.5 we get good approximation values of u(x,t) at ő≤=1.8.
